lessons.title: Comprehensive Review of Basic Syntax
lessons.summary: Review the key points of variables, operators, and control flow. Master the frequently tested patterns on the Silver exam, including operator precedence, short-circuit evaluation, and tricky switch/if patterns.

contents.title: Identifying Pitfalls in Basic Syntax
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- A refresher on [[operator]] precedence and associativity
- Rules for implicit/explicit [[type conversion]]
- Pitfalls in [[for loops]], [[while loops]], and [[switch statements]]

This is a comprehensive review of syntax questions frequently tested on the Silver exam.

contents.title: Operator Precedence and Short-Circuit Evaluation
contents.body:
Operator precedence is a topic frequently targeted on the exam.
Post-increment (i++) returns the value first and then increments; pre-increment (++i) increments first and then returns the value.
The logical operators && and || perform short-circuit evaluation.
If the left operand of && is false, the right operand is not evaluated; if the left operand of || is true, the right operand is not evaluated.
In contrast, & and | are bitwise operators (or non-short-circuit logical operators for boolean types) that always evaluate both operands.
Also be careful not to confuse the assignment operator = with the comparison operator ==.
contents.note: The difference between post-increment and pre-increment becomes apparent when used within expressions. As standalone statements (i++;), both produce the same result.

contents.title: Switch Statement Fall-Through and Patterns
contents.body:
In a switch statement, forgetting a break statement causes "fall-through" to the next case.
This is a very common trick pattern on the exam.
The types that can be used in a [[switch expression]] are byte, short, char, int, [[String]], enum, and their wrapper types.
Remember that long, double, and boolean cannot be used in switch expressions.
The default label can be placed at any position, but it is still affected by fall-through.
Only [[compile]]-time constants ([[literals]] or final variables) can be used as case labels.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Type Conversion and Variable Scope Considerations
contents.body:
Implicit type conversion (widening conversion) occurs automatically from a smaller type to a larger type.
Widening conversion is possible in the order int → long → float → double.
However, precision may be lost in int → float or long → double conversions.
Narrowing conversion (from a larger type to a smaller type) requires an explicit [[cast]].
Loop variables in a for loop are only valid within the for loop's block.
Only boolean types can be used in [[if statement]] conditions — unlike C, integer values cannot be used.
contents.note: The result of byte + byte is of type int. byte b = 1 + 2; is OK because they are compile-time constants, but byte b1 = 1; byte b2 = 2; byte b3 = b1 + b2; is an error.

contents.title: Exam Strategy: Common Basic Syntax Patterns
contents.body:
Basic syntax questions are an area where you should score reliably. Master the following patterns.
| Key Point |
| --- |
| Patterns where post/pre-increment is used inside expressions |
| Patterns where side-effecting expressions are/aren't evaluated due to short-circuit evaluation |
| Fall-through patterns depending on the presence/absence of break in switch statements |
| Situations requiring implicit type conversion vs. explicit casting |
| Variable scope boundaries (for loops, if statements, blocks) |
When in doubt, "think like the compiler" and carefully trace through the code line by line.

quizzes.question: What value is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: x++ is a post-increment, so it first returns the current value of x (3), then x becomes 4. Next, --x is a pre-decrement, so it decrements x from 4 to 3 and then returns 3. Therefore, y = 3 * 3 = 9.

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. Which of the following correctly identifies the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: Since the for loop has no braces {}, its body consists of only the single statement count++;. The System.out.println(i + ": " + count); line is outside the for loop, so variable i is out of scope, resulting in a compile error. Be careful not to be misled by indentation.

choices.text: The for loop condition is invalid
Variable i is referenced outside its scope
There is no semicolon after count++

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: Since num is 5, it matches case 5 and result += "B" is executed. There is no break, so it falls through to case 10, and result += "C" is executed. case 10 has a break so the switch statement is exited. The default and case 1 are not executed. The result is "BC".

lessons.title: Comprehensive Review of OOP
lessons.summary: Review the key concepts of classes, inheritance, polymorphism, abstract classes, and interfaces. Organize the differences between method overriding and overloading, and the behavior of access modifiers.

contents.title: Understanding Object-Oriented Programming Precisely
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How [[inheritance]] and [[constructor]] chaining works
- Rules for [[overriding]] ([[access modifiers]] and return types)
- Type resolution in [[polymorphism]] ([[compile]] time vs. runtime)

The OOP section has the highest score weight on the Silver exam.

contents.title: Requirements for Method Overriding
contents.body:
There are several strict requirements for [[method]] overriding to be valid.
The method name and parameter list (types, count, and order) must match exactly.
The return type must be the same type or a [[covariant return]] type (a sub[[class]] type).
The access modifier must be the same or broader in scope (e.g., changing protected to public is allowed).
Only the same or subclass [[checked exceptions]] can be thrown compared to the parent class.
static/private methods cannot be overridden (in the case of static methods, this is called "hiding").
contents.note: Adding the @Override annotation causes a compile error if the override conditions are not met, helping to prevent mistakes.

contents.title: Polymorphism and Method Invocation
contents.body:
The core of polymorphism is the distinction between the "[[variable]] type" and the "actual object type."
[[Instance]] methods are invoked based on the actual object type (dynamic binding).
Instance variables and [[static methods]] are accessed based on the variable type ([[reference type]]) (static binding).
This distinction is the most frequently tested point on the exam.
Without a [[cast]], you cannot call child class-specific methods from a parent class type variable.
contents.note: Fields are not overridden but "hidden." Which field is visible depends on the reference type.

contents.title: Differences Between Abstract Classes and Interfaces
contents.body:
[[Abstract classes]] can have constructors and can define instance variables and concrete methods.
[[Interface]] fields are implicitly public static final.
Since Java 8, interfaces can define default methods and static methods.
Multiple class inheritance is not allowed, but a class can implement multiple interfaces.
A class that extends an abstract class must implement all abstract methods or itself be declared abstract.
When default methods from interfaces conflict, the implementing class must explicitly override them.
contents.note: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated with new, but their constructors are called via super() from child classes.

contents.title: Exam Strategy: Common OOP Tricks
contents.body:
Watch out for the following tricks in OOP questions.
| Key Point |
| --- |
| The behavioral difference between field hiding and method overriding |
| Confusing overloading (different parameters) with overriding (same parameters) |
| Constructor invocation order in the parent class (super() is implicitly called first) |
| Concrete methods of abstract classes are also inherited |
| Conflict resolution for default methods in interfaces |
When reading code, always trace with awareness of the "declared type (reference type)" vs. the "runtime type."

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: obj.val is a field access, so it references the val of the reference type Base, returning 10. obj.getVal() is an instance method, so the actual object Sub's getVal() is called, returning 20. Fields use static binding while methods use dynamic binding.

quizzes.question: Which of the following statements about method overriding is correct?
quizzes.explanation: When overriding, it is possible to change the access modifier to the same or a broader scope than the parent class. It cannot be narrowed (e.g., public cannot be changed to protected). The return type can be changed to a subclass type (covariant return type). Changing the parameters results in overloading, not overriding.

choices.text: The access modifier can be changed to a narrower scope
The return type can be changed to a superclass type
The access modifier can be changed to a broader scope
Changing the parameter types still counts as overriding

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. Which of the following correctly identifies the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: Since the Bird class is abstract, it does not need to implement Flyable's fly() method. However, the Duck class is a concrete class that extends Bird, so it must implement the fly() method. Since Duck does not have fly(), a compile error occurs.

choices.text: fly() is not implemented in the Bird class
fly() is not implemented in the Duck class
An abstract class cannot implement an interface
The Duck class cannot implement two interfaces

quizzes.question: Select all valid Java access modifiers.
quizzes.explanation: Java has four access modifiers: public, protected, private, and default (package-private). internal is a keyword in Kotlin and C#.

lessons.title: Comprehensive Review of Exceptions and APIs
lessons.summary: Review the key exam-targeted points for the exception hierarchy, try-catch-finally flow, String/StringBuilder behavior, and ArrayList operations.

contents.title: Solidifying Your Score in Exceptions and APIs
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The [[exception]] hierarchy and catch ordering rules
- The execution order of [[try-with-resources]]
- The behavior of key [[methods]] in [[String]]/[[StringBuilder]]/[[ArrayList]]

Let's review the details that are tested on the exam.

contents.title: Execution Flow of try-catch-finally
contents.body:
In try-catch-[[finally]], the finally block is always executed regardless of whether an exception occurs.
Even if there is a return statement in the try block, the finally block is executed first.
If there is a return in the finally block, it overrides the return value from try or catch.
catch blocks are evaluated from top to bottom, and only the first matching catch block is executed.
If a parent [[class]] exception type is written first, the child class catch becomes unreachable, causing a [[compile]] error.
In try-with-resources, the resource's close() method is called before finally.
contents.note: Returning in finally is discouraged, but it is frequently tested on exams. Remember that a return in finally overrides the return from try/catch.

contents.title: String Immutability and StringBuilder Operations
contents.body:
String objects are immutable. String manipulation methods return a new String.
The original String object is not modified, so changes are not reflected unless the return value is assigned to a [[variable]].
StringBuilder is mutable, and methods like append, insert, delete, and reverse modify the object itself.
StringBuilder method chaining is possible because these methods return this (the object itself).
String.concat() and substring() are also frequently tested on exams.
Note that substring(start, end) includes start but excludes end (half-open interval).
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: ArrayList Operations and Considerations
contents.body:
ArrayList<Integer>'s remove method has two [[overloads]].
remove(int index) removes an element by index, and remove(Object o) removes by value.
add(int index, E element) inserts at the specified position, shifting existing elements to the right.
Modifying a list during a for-each loop causes a ConcurrentModificationException.
ArrayList indices start at 0, and out-of-bounds access throws an IndexOutOfBoundsException.
Due to autoboxing, list.remove(2) removes the element at index 2, not the value 2.
contents.note: With ArrayList<Integer>, remove(Integer.valueOf(2)) removes the element with value 2, while remove(2) removes the element at index 2.

contents.title: Exam Strategy: Tips for Exception and API Questions
contents.body:
For exception and API questions, keep the following points in mind.
| Key Point |
| --- |
| Memorize the exception hierarchy (distinguish between checked and unchecked exceptions) |
| Trace the execution order of try-catch-finally precisely |
| Check whether the return value of String methods is being assigned |
| Understand what StringBuilder method chains return |
| Determine whether ArrayList's remove uses index or value |
This knowledge may appear in standalone questions or as part of combined questions.

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: s1.concat(" SE") returns a new String "Java SE" which is assigned to s2. s1 itself remains unchanged as "Java". s1.toUpperCase() returns "JAVA", but since the return value is not assigned to any variable, it has no effect. Therefore, the output is "Java,Java SE".

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: sb.delete(1, 3) deletes from index 1 up to (but not including) index 3, removing indices 1 and 2. "ABCDE" → "ADE". Then insert(2, "XY") inserts "XY" at index 2. "ADE" → "ADXYE". Since StringBuilder is mutable, chained operations can be performed consecutively.

quizzes.question: Which of the following statements about try-catch-finally is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The finally block is always executed regardless of whether an exception occurs. Even if a return statement is executed in the try block, the finally block is executed before returning. The finally block is also executed when an exception is re-thrown in the catch block. However, the finally block is NOT executed if System.exit() is called.

choices.text: The finally block is only executed when an exception occurs
If return is used in the try block, finally is not executed
The finally block is always executed even when System.exit() is called
Even if return is used in the try block, the finally block is still executed

lessons.title: Practice Problem Challenge
lessons.summary: Take on exam-level combined problems that integrate inheritance, exceptions, and polymorphism. Develop code tracing skills and exam time management strategies.

contents.title: Tackling Exam-Level Combined Problems
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to solve combined problems that integrate multiple topics
- Code tracing techniques
- Time allocation strategies to stay calm during the exam

Get a feel for the real exam with practice problems.

contents.title: Combined Patterns: Inheritance and Exceptions
contents.body:
In problems that combine [[inheritance]] and [[exception]] handling, focus on the following points.
Trace the [[constructor]] chain (implicit super() calls) and exception propagation.
The constraints on what exceptions an [[overridden]] [[method]] can throw are also important.
An overriding method in a sub[[class]] cannot throw broader exceptions than the superclass method.
However, [[RuntimeException]] (unchecked [[exceptions]]) are not subject to this constraint.
Also pay attention to the combination of [[finally]] block execution timing and exception re-throwing.
contents.note: Constructors execute in parent-to-child order, while methods are called from the child class due to overriding.

contents.title: Combined Code Tracing Exercise
contents.body:
When tracing code in combined problems, it is essential to follow values step by step.
First, check the [[variable]] declarations and initialization, then trace the method call flow.
When [[polymorphism]] is involved, always be aware of the declared type vs. the actual type.
For state changes inside loops, writing out variable values for each iteration helps track them accurately.
For branches (if/switch), accurately determine the evaluation result of condition expressions.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Exam Time Management Strategy
contents.body:
The Java Silver exam consists of 80 questions in 180 minutes. The target is approximately 2 minutes and 15 seconds per question.
First, skim through all questions to identify easy and difficult ones.
Knowledge questions (such as API specifications) can be solved in 30 seconds if you know them, so tackle those first.
Code tracing questions take more time, so you may want to save them for later.
Spending more than 5 minutes on a difficult question is risky. Flagging it and coming back later is an effective strategy.
Reserve 15-20 minutes for review to check for marking errors and missed details.
contents.note: On the exam, using the process of elimination to rule out "obviously wrong choices" increases your success rate.

contents.title: Final Checklist for Passing the Exam
contents.body:
Review the following items before the exam.
| Checklist Item |
| --- |
| Range and default values of primitive data types (int array default is 0, boolean array is false) |
| Operator precedence and short-circuit evaluation behavior |
| The 5 conditions for overriding (name, parameters, return type, access modifier, exceptions) |
| String immutability and StringBuilder mutability |
| Exception hierarchy (checked vs. unchecked exceptions) |
| Behavior of ArrayList's main methods (add, remove, set, get, size) |
| Differences between abstract classes and interfaces |
Focus your review on weak areas and approach the exam with confidence.

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: The constructor chain of new Z() executes constructors in the order X → Y → Z, outputting "XYZ". obj.show() calls Z's show() method due to polymorphism (since the actual type is Z), outputting "z". The total output is "XYZz".

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. Which of the following correctly identifies the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: a.speak() is declared as potentially throwing the checked exception Exception. The main method has neither a try-catch block nor a throws declaration to handle this checked exception. Therefore, a compile error occurs due to an "unhandled checked exception."

choices.text: The Cat class cannot throw Exception in speak()
A Cat method cannot be called from an Animal type variable
The checked exception is not handled in the main method
The Cat type cannot be cast to the Animal type

quizzes.question: What is output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: In the try block, result += "T" is executed. arr[5] = 10 throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, so result += "U" is skipped. In the catch block, result += "C" is executed. In the finally block, result += "F" is executed. Since the exception was handled by catch, result += "E" is also executed. The result is "TCFE".
